speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] very quiet. How are you doing, Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm OK. I'm alive. I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are alive. I brought some flowers in honor of your anniversary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I brought some flowers so that you smell them and say, I'm here to smell the flowers. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just talking about Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes. In Feldafing, I think from the organization, they sent some money from America from the Joint. And they sent me with some money. I think it was for somebody. This I don't remember exactly how it came about. But I-- I remember transporting a few dollars also for the-- you know, for organizing the departure from Germany. I met a guy there, which later became my closest friend, [? Shia ?] [? Tigger. ?] He was an actor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was organizing a group that goes to Belgium, to Brussels. I guess that was what the money was for, sent from the Joint or from the Jewish Workers Committee or from the [? arbeitering. ?] And he asked me, would you like to join us? We are going to Belgium. So I said, if I may, why not? [? Well, ?] I got nothing in Germany. I don't plan on staying here for the rest of my life. So he told me to go and bring everything that that I have [? in Pferd ?] and come back here to Feldafing-- yeah, I think it was Feldafing-- and we'll go from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me a date that was sometimes in the beginning of February. So I went back to [? Pferd, ?] said goodbye to my friends there, and told them that I finished work. Said, I'm leaving. Wished me good luck and that was it. And I went back to Feldafing. And from there, a group of us by train we went north to Aachen. Aachen is a border town with Belgian on German side. There was a family, a German family, that kept us. And apparently they were connected with the-- with the smugglers that smuggled us over the border. We stayed there a few days until the group of smugglers came for us. And a group maybe of 10 to 12. I don't remember exactly. In fact, there was one lady that was pregnant at the time. Her daughter was born I think in April. And that was February. So she was in her last months. She went with us over the border. We smuggled across the border. Belgium at that time had a policy, or didn't have a policy, with refugees. If they caught you on the border, they arrested you and put you in jail. Or if they caught you on the train from what was it, I think [? Esch ?] was the town, the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: If you went all the way to [? Belgium, ?] they caught you on the train, they gave you a [? jaudam, ?] and took you back to the border. What you did there, they didn't care. But if you managed to smuggle across the border and come into Brussels and go to the police and register, they just asked you, how did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was with me in the case. Smuggled across the border, then a taxi waited for us there. I think they shoved us. It only takes maybe 10 people. I was leaning on the door. When they opened the door, I was asleep, and I fell out of the taxi.

speaker: interviewer
text: That took us to the train. And train took us to Brussels. The next day some friends that lived in Belgium from before the war took us to the police station to register as I had to have some kind of paper. So I remember the policeman asked me, and he translated, how did you come here? So I said by train. So he wrote down by train. And that was it. And I had-- although we had a stamp that [SPEAKING DUTCH] you must immigrate. You-- just-- we were there in transit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Belgium was good. I saw the stores, I couldn't believe it. Oranges in the store, chocolate, you just could go in and buy it if you had the money. Oranges, apples, eggs, who's ever seen anything like that? Free. I still have the taste in my mouth. It tasted so beautiful. Doesn't taste so good now as it tasted then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since I stay there, I have to go to work. I have to find a job. So I found a job in my trade, the fur trade. That was the only thing I knew. And I worked the whole time. We there four and a half years, I worked there the whole time. And of course, I joined back the organization. I was [? escaped, ?] I was already older. I was an adult. It was 1945. I was 18 years old. Joined the organization. And of course, the money that came from the United States helped a lot. They paid our rent, I think, until we got jobs. At a private apartment, you know, one room. We roomed with another friend. In fact, the one that I have here on the photo. I roomed with him. I went to work. We didn't learn much. And after about a few months, I came to Brussels on February 6, I think, 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also went to camps. We had one camp in the spring in [? Tihange. ?] And then we had another camp in Francorchamps in the Spa Mountains. Of course, all paid for by the Joint Distribution Committee or the monies that came from the United States. And I met Regina in November, I think, of 1946. We went out together, and we were married in May of 1947, May 8th. Only a chuppah, not the legal wedding because we didn't have any papers. I didn't have a birth certificate. I think Regina did then. I didn't have any papers at all. And then we registered to come to the United States. But the United States had a quota system. And we were on the Polish quota. Even though I ran away from Poland, I'll be originally born in Poland all of my life. So I was on the Polish quota. At that time, it was very strictly observed. But finally, when-- I think I got some papers from Poland. I think I got my birth certificate. And I had two witnesses from before the war, went to the-- to the police station, to the city hall, we had enough papers to have the legal marriage. So in 1950, I think-- This date, I don't know. I think in June 1950 we had our legal papers. We got the marriage certificate and everything.

words.text: I have to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the same time, a lot of friends went to Australia. They had friends here. And it was easier to go to Australia. But at the same time both papers arrived, the affidavits to come to the United States and to go to Australia. Regina had cousins here. At least two cousins, one that was on TV last night being interviewed, Henry [? Rosenbran. ?] They lived in Kentucky, in Louisville. They sent us the papers. So we asked them, what do you think we should do? He said we can't give you any advice. You'll have to decide it for yourself. I know Australia is just as good as the United States. You've got  to decide.

speaker: interviewee
text: But something decided for me. The war in Korea broke out in 1950. And I figured out in my logic, well, at that time, distance still made a big difference. I looked at a map, Australia was somewhere in the bottom, the other end of the world. And I figured that the United States is gonna be involved no matter how in that war. And Australia might not. And I wanted to be as far away from Europe or anything that has anything to do with wars. And Melbourne was the furthest point in Australia. In fact, that movie from Nevil Shute about the last day of the world was filmed in Melbourne as it being the last city in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Australia. All my friends went there. Family, I didn't have anywhere. So I was with my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Australia 15 years. I worked there. And I was very active in Jewish life, in the Yiddish theatre. I played in the Yiddish theatre very actively for 15 years. We had a very good theatre. Very high standard, professional standard, even though we were amateurs, we had a professional standard because we had two people that were very famous before the war in Yiddish theatre. Jacob Weislitz from the Vilna Troupe, one of the stars living here was there. And one of the great. The first lady of Yiddish theatre, Rachel Holzer. In fact, she's still alive. She's in her late 90s now in Melbourne. And they were leading the leader. They were directing, putting on plays, classics, Yiddish classics, and translations from-- from the Italian theatre, from the American theatre that we played. And that kept me very busy. And also I worked in my trade, worked in the fur trade. Regina had an aunt here that came from Belgium through Israel to the United States. In fact this is an aunt, Regina will tell you about that later, that married us in Brussels. And when she came to the United States, she came to Los Angeles. She kept on bombarding us with letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why should we be separated? You didn't achieve that much in Australia. You didn't become a millionaire. Didn't become rich. Jack is working on a job, you've got two small children, why can't we be together?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, the name Los Angeles at that time had a magic sound, Los Angeles, Hollywood. So in-- I think in 1960 or '59, no, '59 because Sylvia wasn't born yet, we registered in the American Embassy to come here. And since we are again on the Polish quota and it was a five-year wait, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we registered to come to the United States. You know, I don't want to pass by Australia that fast because Australia has a big place in my heart, a very beautiful place in my heart. First of all, this was the first country that opened their doors for us without too many questions. We registered, we went. And all my dearest friends, the ones that are still alive, are there. We were in very close contact with Australia. We already-- already made three trips back there. In 1992 was the last time we were there. And now the friends come here. It's like closer than brothers and sisters. We call each other constantly. And not only did the friendship become [? weaker ?] after we've been here already 30 years, but the friendship enlarged. They over there made friends with my group of friends here so much so that for a wedding of one of the children, they invited some of the friends. And they went together with us to the wedding in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually the family-- this is my family-- it grew bigger and stronger. The ones that are still alive. So many passed away. So many are sick. Listen, we're all not in the autumn, we already have the winter of our lives. So Australia is very important to me emotionally. So we registered in '59 or '58. And then in 1960, Sylvia was born. So we put her on the papers too. What is your first daughter's name? And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dinah. She was named after my mother, Dinah. She was born in 1952 in Melbourne. They were both in Australia. Sylvia was born Silvia [? Oshandless ?] she was called, also after her other grandmother, after my mother's-- after my wife's mother [? Shayla. ?] And you know what, it's funny. When you look at the photos, Sylvia looks just like my wife and like her mother. We have a photo upstairs. You will see it later when you interview Regina from Regina at the cemetery when she was I think five years old. And we have a photo of Sylvia when she was five years old. And it's like one face. And Dinah, she's called after my mother, is the spitting image of my mother. Now I don't remember anymore, but when she was born, when she grew up, I could see my mother in her.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the time came for us to leave, we were a little hesitant. You know, 15 years in Australia, you're established. Yes? What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Australia in the fur trade. I managed a store. Five years, I worked on one job. The first job that I had in a big firm. And then, no, 10 years I worked for [? Davis ?] Brothers. And then the last five years I worked for a guy, he wanted me very much. He had a small store and a small factory. He wanted me to manage the store and the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to work for him.  He was better paying. It was closer to the house. And we needed more money. The family grew. So I worked there. And I did some private work also. But I work very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this probably pushed me to make the decision to leave. I worked that job. It was very responsible because I was responsible for the workers, for everything, for the-- the work to be finished on time, and I couldn't give up the theater, rehearsals. In the beginning, two rehearsals a week, one rehearsal a week, near the end, every day. The last play we did in 1964 it was Sholem Aleichem's And I play the lead. That was around April or May. And in April for the [INAUDIBLE], the [? Wars ?] [? Together ?] [? coronation, ?] April 19, they invited me to perform there. And it was a solo performance. I recited some poetry, which took about 15 minutes. So I learned this and the part there and I couldn't neglect the job. And I remember one time it was before the opening of a play, I went to the-- the general rehearsal in the evening. And 1 o'clock I came back to work because I had to finish off a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Regina was getting furious. She said, you're going to drop that here [? at work, ?] you're crazy. She called me to the factory, are you crazy? You're gonna work day and night? And I realized that something will have to give. You cannot do three things at one time. It is impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that as long as I'm in Melbourne, I cannot-- I won't give up the theatre. I knew I can't-- it's like-- like a disease. So we decided-- and my aunt wrote a letter to us, hey, you were gonna come. She kept an apartment. She had an apartment building with one bedroom apartment. She said, I'm keeping the apartment for you. I'm not renting it out. If you decide you don't want to come, just let me know. She-- at that time, she was in her late 60s. And then I decided after this play, I said no. We got to go. And we decided to come to the United States. We came here on a very famous day, August 14, 1965, in the beginning of the Watts Riots. We came in the middle of the riots. Apparently every time I come from Australia, something happens. In 1992 when we came back from Australia, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we came back in May, the end of April, the middle of the riots in 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, something wrong with me going to Australia. Every time I come from Australia-- I'm in Hawaii on the stop to come here, I hear about the riots in Los Angeles. And we came here in '65 and we sort of established ourselves. I didn't go back to the fur trade. I didn't want to go back. It's dirty. It's-- I didn't want to work in it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a letter of recommendation to a company to a gentleman, [? Sig Issacs. ?] He had a children's and ladies' manufacturing company, huge national company. It went all over the states. A friend, a mutual friend, gave me his address, his telephone. I called him up. As soon as I called him, he came over the next day in a huge, white Lincoln. I mean, it took up half street.

speaker: interviewer
text: I looked at him, oh my god. Never seen anything like it. I mean in Australia, you weren't used to these big cars. I got into the car. And I begged him, could you turn that thing off? I nearly froze to death from the air conditioning. I wasn't used to that. I drove a car in Melbourne, wasn't like that. And he said to me, look, why don't you come down, see what I do, and then you decide if you want a job, a job I'll give you right away. When I told my aunt that I know a guy here, a millionaire, he's a multi-millionaire, he wants to give me a job. She said, you didn't take it yet? I said, I know know. Should I? Are you crazy? You are out of your mind? Somebody wants to give you a job and you're doubting? See, I had no problem with the language. I spoke English we when came, of course with the English accent, which you see I got rid of. And sure enough I took the job as a shipping clerk. And I worked there five years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went out on my own. It was very interesting, very interesting. I learned Los Angeles. I learned the United States. For me, it was an education that I couldn't believe. I remember writing the letters to Melbourne about Los Angeles, about the United States. I said it's very similar. It's practically the same with one difference. Melbourne, Australia is the kindergarten. This is the college for grown ups, the rough college of grown ups. That's-- they're sort of like 25 years ahead, not in technology but in attitude and lifestyle. It's a completely different thing. And of course, I had to learn how to drive on the right side of the street, which was very funny. At times it wasn't so funny. I remember driving out on Olympic one day on the left side with Sylvia in the car and Dinah. And Dinah starts coming, Daddy, you're in the wrong side! Didn't realize. But I learned very quick. I became accustomed to it. It was very tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: I cried. I remember getting off the bus, and I cried my eyes out. Why did I do it? Why did I come to this jungle? But I-- I'm glad that I realized that it is a beginning. No matter where you make your beginning, it is tough. And the easy thing of getting accustomed to living in LA was the fact that whoever I talked to, they ask me, where you come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said from Australia. Oh, yeah, I came from New York. I came from Chicago. I mean, it was no big deal. I wasn't such an exceptional newcomer. Everybody here was a newcomer from somewhere. And they all had it tough. That made it psychologically a little easier for us to get accustomed. It was tough. In the very first few months, Regina was sick. She had to have surgery. She'll tell you about that. That was very, very tough for us. The kids were sick. We lived in a one bedroom apartment. But within a year and a half, and I made friends right away, I had a group of friends. And I met this guy that I told you about before that I met in [? Pferd, ?] when I went to work in this American kitchen at the airport, [? Nate ?] [? Gipsman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: He lived here in LA. He came from Detroit here. And I met him. And it was quite a reunion. At that time, it was after what? It was 1950. And I saw him in-- no, not-- 1965, 20 years later. And we renewed our friendship. And we're close friends until now.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a help. I had people around me right away. There was a telephone. I met a lady, in fact, this lady's being interviewed now at this moment also, [? Lillian ?] [? Meisner. ?] She's very active. She's a teacher. She's a friend of mine from school from before the war. This made it a lot easier than it would have been, even though I cried my eyes out missing my friends in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: To show you how things are, I went out and saw the big cars. And I said, my god, what's the matter with these Americans? They cannot drive human-sized car? They have to drive this big, showoff cars? Six months later, I bought a car that was just as big. I was afraid to drive a small car here, you know, out on the freeways, out everywhere. This is how you change.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is the bloody sun out again. We lived in Melbourne where the season changed three times in one day. The reason-- a little part of the reason why we left was the climate. I was mad here. The sun is out again. That's the way you think. Something that you don't want to like. But slowly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things change. I remember getting a letter when the kids were so sick and when it was a week before Regina went to hospital. And we had a friend of ours here from Australia. It was a travel agent. And he saw the situation. Apparently, he went back to Melbourne and he told them. And within two days or three days, I received a letter. Jack, come back home. There will be no question asked. We are sending you out the tickets. Come back home. There will be a bottle of Johnny Walker waiting for you on the table. And come back home. You don't need that. It wasn't even tempting. I wrote to them. I thanked them very much. And some of them were insulted. I said to them, I'll have to give myself a chance. If coming here was a mistake, I don't want to make a second mistake. If coming was a mistake, I think going back is going to be a bigger mistake. I'm not coming back to feed it. I got to stick it out. And the opportunities to freedom, all that. But for the kids, it was not a problem. Sylvia was four and half, five years old. She was-- she's-- If she comes here later you'll see, she's very outgoing. She followed the cat into a house. And she went in and she said, my name is Sylvia Lewin. I came from Australia. Right away she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: For Dinah it was tough. Dinah was 13. She started in the junior high, right? Yeah, 13 going to junior high. You know, girls have cliques, groups. They teased her about her English accent, the way she was dressed. In Australia, she had to wear a uniform in junior high. So she was dressed conservatively, very well-mannered, English style, very conservative.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they teased-- and she-- Dinah must have suffered much more than we know in the beginning. It was very hard for her. Very hard. But she grew out of it too. Time takes care of everything. And the longer I was here, I became very active here in Jewish cultural life. We have the Yiddish group here, the Yiddish cultural club here, where I perform once in a while with poetry readings, with readings from Jewish literature. That's all I do. I don't want to get more involved in theater or anything. I could maybe if I wanted, but I don't have the urge for that. What I do I'm satisfied with. After working five years for [? Sig ?] [? Issacs' ?] company. And I used to take things from there like buying. They gave me a discount, all very cheap, children's dresses or ladies' dresses, slightly damaged or more damaged. And I used to sell it around the house to friends. And they told me there in the shipping department, a cousin of his was-- of [? Sig ?] [? Issacs ?] was the manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, listen, if you can sell it here, why don't you take more? You can get as much merchandise as you want. Why don't you sell? And it was very pleasant to sell something, you buy something for $2 and you sell it $4. I've never done that before. I was always working for somebody all my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a couple of friends introduced me to the swap meets. And I went out a couple of times with them. And for about a year or six months, I used to go on the weekends. And then, again, it became too much, seven days a week. It became too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew this isn't the kind of thing that you discuss with your whole family. I have to make that decision myself. One day, I think it was before the Jewish New Year, and had to been in either '69 or '70, I said to Regina, I got something to tell you. So she said, that you gave notice? She knew right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said yes. I gave notice at work. I said that I wanted [? to walk. ?] And I asked Mr. [? Isaacs, ?] I said I know I had credit with you now. You gave me whatever I wanted to while I worked for you. And I paid every week. He said to me, Jack, the sky's the limit. Whatever you want for as long as you want. If you need it, just come tell Eli. He was the manager. He picked up the phone, called him up right away, whenever Jack comes in, whatever he wants, give him. Don't worry about money. And that helped me a lot. And we parted very good friends. In fact, he invited me at his silver wedding anniversary with all dignitaries. He was a big shot. He invited us. And then I started buying from different people. And I did it for 20 years, from 1970 till 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have a minute tape left.

speaker: interviewer
text: I feel like--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was good?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.
